title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL
TAX APPEALS NO. E785 OF 2025
ALMAADAAR TRADING COMPANY LIMITED………………….
APPELLANT
-VERSUS-
THE COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES……………….…
RESPONDENT
JUDGEMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a duly registered Taxpayer.
2. The Respondent is established under the Kenya Revenue
Authority Act, Cap 469 Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “the Act”).
Under Section 5 (1), the Respondent is an agency of the
Government for the collection and receipt of all revenue.
Further, under Section 5 (2) with respect to the performance of
its function under subsection (1), the Respondent is mandated
to administer and enforce all provisions of the written laws as
set out in Part 1 & 2 of the First Schedule to the Act for the
purposes of assessing, collecting and accounting for all
revenues in accordance with those laws.
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
1

3. The Respondent issued an additional VAT assessment on 12th
June 2024 for the period August 2023 amounting to Kshs.
1,596,016.49.
4. The Appellant did not object within the statutory period of thirty
(30) days as required under Section 51(2) of the Tax Procedures
Act, CAP 469B of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “TPA”).
5. The Appellant on 19th June 2025, lodged an application for
extension of time to file a late objection, citing “absence from
Kenya.”
6. The Respondent in response to the Appellants application by an
electronic mail dated 26th June 2025, requested supporting
documentation.
7. The Respondent states that the Appellant did not provide any
documentary evidence in support of its late objection
8. Consequently, the Respondent rejected the application on 2nd
July 2025 for lack of merit.
9. The Appellant, being aggrieved by the Respondent’s decision,
filed the present appeal before the Tribunal on 22nd July 2025.
THE APPEAL
10. The Appellant lodged memorandum of appeal dated and filed
on 22nd July 2025 raising the following grounds of appeal:
(i) That the Respondent erred in fact and law in rejecting the
late objection application to the Value Added Tax (VAT)
Assessment.
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
2

(ii) That the Appellant does not agree to the amount payable
as per the late objection rejection notice issued by the
Respondent and wishes to avail the grounds for late
objection.
(iii) That it is ready and willing to avail documents in support
of the VAT inputs disallowed by the Respondent during the
assessment.
(iv) That the Appellant submits that the objection rejection
notice issued by the Respondent to confirm the
assessment is unjust, unfair, and does not reflect the
accurate and right tax payable, as the Appellant was not
accorded a fair hearing by the Respondent.
(v) That the Appellant prayed that the Tribunal would review
the appeal on its merit and restrain the Respondent from
enforcing the late objection rejection notice dated 2nd July
2025.
THE APPELLANT’S CASE
11. The Appellant’s case is premised on the Memorandum of
Appeal dated 22nd July 2025 filed on even date and the
statement of facts dated 22nd July 2025 filed on even date.
12. On 13th March 2025, the Respondent issued a Value Added Tax
(VAT) assessment against the Appellant amounting to Kshs.
1,596,016.49 for August 2023.
13. On 19th June 2025 the Appellant objected to the assessments
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
3

through i-Tax and stated that the grounds of the late objection
were due to the director’s absence from Kenya.
14. The Respondent requested the Appellant via electronic mail on
26th June 2025 to submit supporting documents via electronic
mail. The Appellant, however, was not accorded sufficient time
to submit the support documents to justify the late objection
application; The Appellant was granted 3 working days to
submit the support documents and respond to the electronic
mail communication.
15. On 2nd July 2025, the Respondent rejected the late objection
application, citing the lack of merit.
16. The Respondent failed to grant a fair hearing to the Appellant
by rejecting the late objection application within 3 working
days from the date of the request for support documents and 2
days before the lapse of 14 days from the date of lodging the
late objection application.
17. In light of the foregoing, the Appellant prays that the Tribunal
be pleased to set aside the Respondent’s late objection
rejection notice dated 2nd July 2025 in its entirety.
18. The Appellant stated that at the hearing of the Appeal, that it
hall provide oral and documentary evidence to support the
grounds for the late objection application and support the input
VAT claim that the Respondent disallowed in raising the
assessment.
19. The Appellant stated that it would be willing for the matter to
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
4

be resolved under Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR).
Appellant’s Prayers
20. The Appellant prayed that the Tribunal would review the
appeal on its merit and restrain the Respondent from enforcing
the late objection rejection notice dated 2nd July 2025.
RESPONDENT’S CASE
21. The Respondent’s case is premised on the Statement of facts
dated 23rd September 2024 and filed on the even date.
22. The Respondent stated that it undertook a desk review to
establish the accuracy of the Income Tax returns and Value-
Added Returns filed by the Appellant
23. The Respondent stated that the review established that for
the period August 2023 the Appellant had made supplies to
several entities, namely: Hamisi Constituency Development
Fund (CDF), and Kenya Civil Aviation Authority, for which no
corresponding sales had been made in the VAT Return.
24. The Respondent stated that it established that the Appellant
had claimed VAT from several supplies which it was required to
provide proof of the supplies which the Appellant did not
respond leading to an upload of the demand in the iTax
System for the Period 1st August to 31st August 2023.
25. The Respondent stated that the Appellant did not object to the
Assessments within the prescribed timelines and it was only on
19th June 2025 when it made the application for late objection
on grounds of absence from Kenya.
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
5

26. The Respondent stated that via an email it wrote to the
Appellant on 26th June 2025 requesting the Appellant to
provide evidence to support the ground advanced for the later
objection.
27. The Respondent stated that it considered all the information
availed to them by the Appellant and issued a response to the
Request for extension of time to lodge a late objection dated
2nd July 2025 explaining its reasons for rejecting the Appellant's
late application.
28. The Respondent stated that, having carefully considered the
grounds raised in the Appeal, the sole issue for determination
is whether it was justified in rejecting the Appellant’s
application for extension of time to lodge a late objection to
the Value Added Tax (VAT) assessment. The Respondent was
of the view that the Appeal is confined strictly to the propriety
of the late objection rejection and does not extend to the
merits of the underlying assessment.
29. The Respondent affirmed that the law imposes a clear
obligation upon a taxpayer not only to lodge a valid objection
within the prescribed statutory timelines but also, where an
application for late objection is made, to ensure that such
application is fully supported by cogent evidence. The
Respondent stated that in the present case, the assessment
was issued on 12th June 2024, yet the Appellant only filed an
application for late objection on 19th June 2025, a delay of
approximately one year, and merely cited “absence from
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
6

Kenya” without providing any supporting documentation.
30. The Respondent was of the view that this position falls
squarely within the ambit of Section 51(7) of the TPA, which
sets out strict and conjunctive conditions that must be satisfied
before an extension of time can be granted. The Respondent
affirmed that the provision requires a taxpayer to demonstrate
both that they were prevented from lodging the objection due
to absence from Kenya, sickness, or other reasonable cause,
and that they did not unreasonably delay in lodging the
objection. The Respondent stated that these conditions must
be read together and not disjunctively.
31. The Respondent further affirmed that this provision must be
read alongside Section 56(1) of the Tax Procedures Act, which
places the burden of proof squarely on the taxpayer to
demonstrate that a tax decision is incorrect. In this regard, the
Respondent was of the view that the Appellant could not
merely allege absence from Kenya but was required to provide
documentary evidence substantiating the claim and to explain
the delay in a satisfactory and comprehensive manner.
32. The Respondent stated that the delay of over one year was
manifestly inordinate and remained wholly unexplained. The
Respondent affirmed that the Appellant failed to indicate which
of its directors was absent from Kenya, the duration of such
absence, and why no other director or officer of the company
could undertake the statutory obligation of lodging an
objection within time. The Respondent was of the view that the
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
7

failure to provide such critical details rendered the application
deficient and unsupported.
33. The Respondent further stated that, in the interest of fairness
and procedural propriety, it did not summarily dismiss the
application but instead issued a notice on 26th June 2025
requesting the Appellant to provide evidence in support of the
late objection application. The Respondent affirmed that this
step was taken to accord the Appellant an opportunity to
substantiate its claims.
34. However, the Respondent was of the view that it was
constrained by Section 51(7A) of the Tax Procedures Act,
which mandates the Commissioner to render a decision within
fourteen days of receipt of the application. The Respondent
stated that this statutory limitation meant that it could not
grant the Appellant additional time beyond what is provided in
law and was therefore required to determine the application
based on the material available. Consequently, the
Respondent issued its decision on 2nd July 2025 within the
prescribed timelines.
35. The Respondent affirmed that despite indicating a willingness
to provide documentation, the Appellant failed to avail any
evidence both at the objection stage and before the Tribunal.
The Respondent was of the view that in the absence of such
documentation, there is no basis upon which the Tribunal can
evaluate or fault the Respondent’s decision, and accordingly,
the Appeal is devoid of merit.
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
8

36. On the issue of the accuracy of the tax assessment, the
Respondent stated that the same does not properly arise for
determination in the present Appeal, as the dispute is limited
to the rejection of the late objection application. Nevertheless,
the Respondent affirmed that the assessment was lawfully
issued pursuant to Section 31(1) of the Tax Procedures Act,
which empowers the Commissioner to amend an assessment
based on available information and to the best of its judgment
to ensure that the correct amount of tax is assessed.
37. The Respondent was of the view that it acted within its
statutory mandate in determining the tax liability and issuing
the assessment, particularly in light of the Appellant’s failure
to file accurate returns or provide supporting documentation.
The Respondent further affirmed that the Appellant has not,
even at the appellate stage, provided any documentation to
support its claimed input VAT or to challenge the assessment.
38. The Respondent stated that in the absence of a valid and
supported objection, the assessed VAT liability of Kshs.
1,596,016.49 remains lawfully due and payable.
39. With regard to the allegation that the Appellant was denied a
fair hearing, the Respondent was of the view that this
contention is misplaced and unfounded. The Respondent
affirmed that this issue does not arise from the decision under
appeal, which was limited strictly to the rejection of the late
objection application. The Respondent further stated that
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
9

under Section 24(2) of the TPA it is not bound by the
taxpayer’s returns and is empowered to assess tax liability
using any available information.
40. The Respondent affirmed that it acted within its statutory
powers under Section 59(1) of the TPA in requiring the
Appellant to furnish documents and information necessary to
determine its tax liability. The Respondent was of the view that
the Appellant failed to comply with these statutory
requirements despite several opportunities and
correspondences.
41. The Respondent reiterated that under Section 56(1) of the TPA,
the burden of proof lies with the taxpayer, and the Appellant’s
failure to provide the requested documentation meant that it
failed to discharge this burden. The Respondent therefore
affirmed that the allegations raised in the Memorandum of
Appeal are unsupported in law and fact.
42. In conclusion, the Respondent was of the view that the Appeal
is fundamentally flawed, lacks evidentiary support, and does
not meet the legal threshold required to overturn the
Respondent’s decision.
Respondent’s Prayers
43. The Respondent prayed that the Tribunal would dismiss the
appeal with costs to it and its late objection rejection notice
dated 22nd July 2025 be upheld.
PARTIES SUBMISSIONS
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
10

44. The parties herein did not file submissions despite being
directed by the Tribunal on 26th February 2026.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
45. The Tribunal having carefully considered the filings made by
the parties is of the considered view that the Appeal herein
distils into the following single issue which commends for its
determination:
Whether the Respondent’s decision rejecting the Appellant’s
application for extension of time to lodge a late objection is
proper and sustainable in law.
ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS
46. The Tribunal proceeds on the settled position that the present
Appeal arises from the Respondent’s decision rejecting the
Appellant’s application for extension of time to lodge a late
objection, and therefore confines itself to determining whether
that decision was properly made within the framework of the
law.
47. The statutory position is clear. Section 51(2) of the TPA
requires a taxpayer to lodge a notice of objection within thirty
(30) days of being notified of a tax decision. This requirement
is couched in mandatory terms and goes to the root of the
objection process.
48. Where a taxpayer fails to comply with this requirement, the
law provides a limited exception under Section 51(7) of the
TPA which permits the Commissioner to extend time only
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
11

where the taxpayer demonstrates that the delay was
occasioned by absence from Kenya, sickness, or other
reasonable cause, and that there was no unreasonable delay.
The use of the word “and” in the provision makes it clear that
these conditions are conjunctive and must be satisfied
simultaneously.
49. This interpretation has been affirmed in Equity Group
Holdings Limited v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes
[2021] eKLR, where it was held that failure to satisfy either
limb is fatal to an application for extension of time.
50. In the present case, the Appellant attributed the delay to the
averred absence from Kenya of its director. However, the
Tribunal notes that no documentary evidence was provided to
support this assertion. The Appellant did not identify the
director in question, did not provide any travel records, and did
not explain the duration of the alleged absence or why the
company could not act through its other officers.
51. Further, the delay of approximately one year remains wholly
unexplained and is, in the Tribunal’s view, manifestly
inordinate. The requirement that delay be satisfactorily
explained has been emphasized in Stanbic Bank Kenya
Limited v Kenya Revenue Authority [2019] eKLR, where
it was held that a party seeking extension of time must place
sufficient material before the decision-maker to justify the
exercise of discretion. Similarly, in Nicholas Kiptoo Arap
Korir Salat v IEBC & 7 Others [2014] eKLR, the Supreme
Court underscored that extension of time is not a right but an
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
12

equitable remedy that must be earned through the
demonstration of sufficient cause.
52. The Tribunal therefore finds that the Appellant failed to
demonstrate both the existence of a reasonable cause and the
absence of unreasonable delay as required under Section 51(7)
of the TPA. Additionally, Section 56(1) of the TPA places the
burden of proof squarely upon the taxpayer to demonstrate
that a tax decision is incorrect. This principle has been
consistently upheld, including in Commissioner of Domestic
Taxes v Total Touch Cargo Holland [2018] eKLR, where it
was held that the taxpayer must rebut the Commissioner’s
position with cogent evidence.
53. In the instant case, the Appellant failed to provide any
documentary evidence in support of its application, failed to
substantiate the reasons advanced for the delay, and failed to
demonstrate any error in the Respondent’s decision. The
Tribunal therefore finds that the Appellant did not discharge
the statutory burden of proof.
54. In the absence of a valid and properly supported objection,
the assessment issued by the Respondent remains final. As
was held in Commissioner of Domestic Taxes v Unga
Limited [2020] eKLR, where no valid objection exists, the
assessment cannot be reopened through an appeal.
55. In view of the foregoing analysis, the applicable statutory
provisions, and the cited authorities, the Tribunal finds and
holds that the Appellant has failed to demonstrate that the
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
13

Respondent’s decision rejecting the late objection application
was erroneous in law or fact, and accordingly failed to
discharge the burden placed upon it under Section 56(1) of the
TPA.
FINAL DECISION
56. The upshot to the foregoing is that the Tribunal finds and holds
that the Appeal fails and it proceeds to make the following
Orders:
(a) The Appeal be and is hereby dismissed.
(b) The Respondent late objection rejection notice dated 22nd
July 2025 be and is hereby upheld.
(c) Each party to bear its own cost.
57. It is so Ordered.
DATED and DELIVERED at NAIROBI on this 27th day of March,
2026.
………………………………….
CHRISTINE A. MUGA
CHAIRPERSON
………………………….. …………….
……………..
DR. TIMOTHY VIKIRU
BERNADETTE GITARI
MEMBER
MEMBER
………….…..…………… ……….……..
…………….
DOMINIC K. RONO BILLY MIJUNGU
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
14

MEMBER
MEMBER
JUDGEMENT-TAT NO E785 OF 2025- ALMAADAR TRADING COMPANY Vs COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
15
